instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Maturity Benefit of ULIP Plans
Unit-Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs)1 are market-linked life insurance plans that offer two major benefits –
They help you earn market-linked returns and build up a corpus, which comes with associated risks in the market.
They provide life insurance protection during the policy tenure
This means that when you invest in ULIPs, your premium is directed toward the market-linked funds that you choose. Your investment, then, yields returns depending on the performance of the selected fund & market conditions, among several other factors.
For instance, if you have invested in an equity fund and the equity markets are on the rise, your investment would also grow. Similarly, if the equity market goes down, the investment would also suffer. Over the policy tenure, your premium is accumulated into the fund value, which moves in tandem with the market. This helps you create a market-linked corpus for your financial goals where the returns depend on the market movements and the underlying fund chosen by the policyholder.
In the event of your death during the policy term, ULIPs pay out a death benefit, that is, the sum assured under the plan, to your nominees. Upon policy maturity, ULIPs pay out the fund value as a maturity benefit as per the policy terms & conditions. ULIPs also allow the benefits of partial withdrawals, switching, premium redirection, top-up premiums, etc., so that you can manage your investments per your needs. But do you know the benefits that ULIPs pay?
Let’s understand.
Meaning of ULIP Maturity Benefits
The maturity benefit of a ULIP refers to the fund value payable to the policyholder if the life assured  the policy term. This amount is based on the market performance of the chosen funds, net of applicable charges. ULIPs allow policyholders to combine life insurance protection with long-term wealth creation, helping them build a corpus to meet important financial goals. 1
Benefits Under ULIPs
Here’s a detailed look at the features and benefits of ULIPs:
Partial Withdrawal
ULIPs allow partial withdrawals after the mandatory 5-year lock-in period in case of exigencies. Policyholders can access a part of the fund's value while the remaining investment earns market-linked returns. 2 3
Flexibility
ULIPs offer flexibility through fund switching, allowing policyholders to adjust their allocations, risk exposure, and growth strategies in response to market performance and personal goals. 4
Tax Benefits
The premiums you pay for a life insurance policy, including ULIPs, are eligible for tax deduction under Section 80C*, up to ₹1.5 lakh in a financial year, subject to applicable conditions. The death benefit received by the nominee is fully tax-exempt under Section 10(10D). Maturity proceeds may also be exempt under Section 10(10D), provided the policy complies with the prescribed tax rules on premium thresholds and policy structure. 5
*Section 80C deductions are available only under the old tax regime.
Core ULIP Components
Understanding the following core elements of a ULIP can help individuals evaluate coverage, growth potential, and risk, enabling them to make informed decisions about policy structure and investment strategy.
Sum assured
The sum assured in a ULIP is expressed as a multiple of the premium paid2. For instance, if you pay a premium of Rs.1 lakh and the sum assured is 10 times the premium, you will get a coverage of Rs.10 lakhs.
Fund Value
The fund value represents the overall value of your investments after factoring in the ULIP charges, and the returns earned. In simpler terms, the fund value is calculated by multiplying the per unit price (current NAV) by the number of units in your fund3. Now that you know these terms, let’s understand the benefits paid under ULIPs.
Death Benefit
Since ULIP is a type of life insurance plan, it covers the risk of a premature demise. If the life insured happens to pass away during the policy tenure, ULIPs pay the death benefit. This benefit is expressed as the higher of the sum assured or the available fund value as of the date of death4 under some ULIP plans. This will vary from plan to plan and company to company.However, there are other types of ULIPs too, called Sum Assured plus Fund Value ULIPs5, wherein the death benefit is calculated by adding up the sum assured and the fund value5.For instance, say Mr Sharma bought a ULIP paying an annual premium of Rs.1 lakh. The sum assured is Rs.10 lakhs. Mr Sharma died in the 10th year of the policy when the fund value was Rs.12 lakhs. In this case, ULIPs will usually pay the higher of the sum assured or the fund value, which is Rs.12 lakhs, as a death benefit. However, if Mr Sharma had opted for the Sum Assured plus Fund Value ULIP, the death benefit would be Rs.22 lakhs (Rs.10 lakhs + Rs.12 lakhs).
What Happens to ULIPs After Maturity?
Once the plan matures, the coverage stops. You get the maturity benefits and the coverage is terminated. However, if you have chosen the settlement option, your fund value stays invested in the selected funds. The coverage does not continue but you can earn market-linked investment returns over the next five years when you withdraw the corpus systematically under the settlement option.
How to Claim a Maturity Benefit?
In the case of maturity, the insurance company usually starts preparing for the claim payment before the maturity date. On the day of maturity the company will process the maturity amount and initiate the payment. Ensure your bank details are recorded with the insurer for hassle-free payout8.
Taxation on Maturity & Death Benefit7
The death benefit paid out under a ULIP plan is exempt from taxation.
The maturity benefit of a ULIP has specific taxation rules that you need to know. Here are the applicable rules –
If you had bought the ULIP before 1st February 2021, the maturity proceeds would be tax-free if the premium is up to 10% of the death sum assured—section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961 grants this exemption.
If you bought the ULIP on or after 1st February 2021, the maturity benefit would be tax-free, provided the aggregate annual premium under all ULIPs is up to Rs.2.5 lakhs and policy is satisfying Section 10(10D) conditions as mentioned there under.
If the annual premium exceeds Rs.2.5 lakhs and the ULIP is bought on or after 1st February 2021, long term capital gains tax would apply. Here’s how –
If you had invested the premium in debt-oriented funds, the earned returns would be taxed at 20% without the benefit of indexation.
For equity-oriented funds, tax exemption is allowed for returns going up to Rs.1 lakh. Returns over Rs.1 lakh are taxed at 10%.
So, understand the death and maturity benefits payable under ULIPs and their tax implications. The death benefit is always tax-free irrespective of the premium amount7, while the maturity benefit might be taxable depending on your policy details. So, know the benefits so that you can plan for your financial goals accordingly.
Rules Regarding the Maturity Benefits of a ULIP
Besides the tax regulation, other factors also govern the maturity benefits of ULIPs in India:
Lock-In Period: ULIPs have a mandatory lock-in period of five years, during which policyholders are not permitted to partially withdraw funds. It encourages long-term investment, allowing the fund value to grow with market-linked returns. 6
Revival of Discontinued Policies: - If the policy is discontinued within first three years due to non-payment of premiums for three consecutive years from inception, the insurance cover ceases immediately. The insurer may allow revival within the permitted period. If the policy is not revived, the surrender value is paid at the end of the third policy anniversary or after the revival period, whichever is later. If the policy is discontinued after three years then the policy is not revived within the allowed revival period, it is terminated and the surrender value is paid. The insurer may, if opted by the policyholder, continue the risk cover by deducting applicable charges until the fund value falls to one year’s premium. Once the fund value equals one year’s premium, the policy is terminated and the fund value is paid. If policies are with a 5-year lock-in (policies issued on or after 01-09-2010) then on premium discontinuance, the policyholder may either revive the policy or opt for complete withdrawal without risk cover. The insurer will notify these options within 15 days of grace period expiry. If no option or withdrawal is chosen within 30 days, the proceeds are paid only after completion of the lock-in period. If discontinuance occurs during the lock-in period, the policy can be revived within two years from discontinuance, subject to completion of the lock-in period.
Key Takeaways
Upon maturity, ULIPs provide policyholders with the accumulated fund value, which can be received as a lump sum or in regular intervals.
Tax implications vary: Death benefits are always exempt from tax. However, maturity benefits are tax-free only if the policy satisfies the premium-to-sum-assured conditions mentioned in Section 10(10D). 7
ULIPs offer flexibility through partial withdrawals, fund switching, , allowing individuals to manage risk and optimize returns according to market conditions. 8
Conclusion
When a ULIP reaches maturity, both the life insurance coverage and the market-linked investment component come to an end. After maturity, only the fund value is payable (either as a lump sum or through the settlement option). No life cover continues beyond that point. Policyholders need to assess these points to make the best use of ULIPs and strengthen their overall financial planning.
FAQs
1. Can I stay invested in ULIP after maturity?
You can’t stay invested in a ULIP (Unit Linked Insurance Plan) after it matures. Once the policy reaches its maturity date, it ends, and the accumulated fund value is paid to you. However, you can withdraw the maturity benefit under ULIPs in a phased manner by choosing the settlement option wherein the maturity benefit is periodically paid over a period of time (which shall not exceed 5 years from the date of maturity).
2. What is the return of ULIP after 10 years?
ULIP returns over 10 years largely depend on the type of market-linked fund you choose and how the market performs during that time. Equity funds generally have the potential to give higher returns. Still, they come with higher risk, while debt funds offer more stability and lower risk, making them suitable for risk-averse individuals.
Disclaimer
IN THIS POLICY, THE INVESTMENT RISK IN INVESTMENT PORTFOLIO IS BORNE BY THE POLICYHOLDER
The Unit Linked Insurance Products do not offer any liquidity during the first five years of the contract. The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year.
ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. The various funds offered under this contract are the names of the funds and do not in any way indicate the quality of these plans, their future prospects and returns.
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. For more details on risk factors, terms and conditions please read the sales brochure & policy document (available on www.bajajlifeinsurance.com) carefully before concluding a sale. CIN: U66010PN2001PLC015959,  call us on Customer Care No. 020-6712 1212 , mail us on: customercare@bajajlife.com. The Logo of Bajaj Life Insurance Limited is provided on the basis of license given by Bajaj Finserv Ltd. to use its “Bajaj” Logo.

output.final_text:
Maturity Benefit of ULIP Plans
Unit-Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs)1 are market-linked life insurance plans that offer two major benefits –
● They help you earn market-linked returns and build up a corpus, which comes with
associated risks in the market.
● They provide life insurance protection during the policy tenure
This means that when you invest in ULIPs, your premium is directed toward the market-linked funds that
you choose. Your investment, then, yields returns depending on the performance of the selected fund &
market conditions, among several other factors.
For instance, if you have invested in an equity fund and the equity markets are on the rise, your investment
would also grow. Similarly, if the equity market goes down, the investment would also suffer. Over the
policy tenure, your premium is accumulated into the fund value, which moves in tandem with the market.
This helps you create a market-linked corpus for your financial goals where the returns depend on the
market movements and the underlying fund chosen by the policyholder.
In the event of your death during the policy term, ULIPs pay out a death benefit, that is, the sum assured
under the plan, to your nominees. Upon policy maturity, ULIPs pay out the fund value as a maturity benefit
as per the policy terms & conditions. ULIPs also allow the benefits of partial withdrawals, switching,
premium redirection, top-up premiums, etc., so that you can manage your investments per your needs.
But do you know the benefits that ULIPs pay?
Let’s understand.
Meaning of ULIP Maturity Benefits
The maturity benefit of a ULIP refers to the fund value payable to the policyholder if the life assured the
policy term. This amount is based on the market performance of the chosen funds, net of applicable
charges. ULIPs allow policyholders to combine life insurance protection with long-term wealth creation,
helping them build a corpus to meet important financial goals. 1
Benefits Under ULIPs
Here’s a detailed look at the features and benefits of ULIPs:
Partial Withdrawal
ULIPs allow partial withdrawals after the mandatory 5-year lock-in period in case of exigencies.
Policyholders can access a part of the fund's value while the remaining investment earns market-linked
returns. 2 3
Flexibility
ULIPs offer flexibility through fund switching, allowing policyholders to adjust their allocations, risk
exposure, and growth strategies in response to market performance and personal goals. 4
Tax Benefits
The premiums you pay for a life insurance policy, including ULIPs, are eligible for tax deduction under
Section 80C*, up to ₹1.5 lakh in a financial year, subject to applicable conditions. The death benefit
received by the nominee is fully tax-exempt under Section 10(10D). Maturity proceeds may also be
exempt under Section 10(10D), provided the policy complies with the prescribed tax rules on premium
thresholds and policy structure. 5
*Section 80C deductions are available only under the old tax regime.
Core ULIP Components
Understanding the following core elements of a ULIP can help individuals evaluate coverage, growth
potential, and risk, enabling them to make informed decisions about policy structure and investment
strategy.
1. Sum assured
The sum assured in a ULIP is expressed as a multiple of the premium paid2. For instance,
if you pay a premium of Rs.1 lakh and the sum assured is 10 times the premium, you will get a
coverage of Rs.10 lakhs.
2. Fund Value
The fund value represents the overall value of your investments after factoring in the
ULIP charges, and the returns earned. In simpler terms, the fund value is calculated by
multiplying the per unit price (current NAV) by the number of units in your fund3. Now that you
know these terms, let’s understand the benefits paid under ULIPs.
3. Death Benefit
Since ULIP is a type of life insurance plan, it covers the risk of a premature demise. If the
life insured happens to pass away during the policy tenure, ULIPs pay the death benefit. This
benefit is expressed as the higher of the sum assured or the available fund value as of the date
of death4 under some ULIP plans. This will vary from plan to plan and company to company.
However, there are other types of ULIPs too, called Sum Assured plus Fund Value ULIPs5,
wherein the death benefit is calculated by adding up the sum assured and the fund value5.
For instance, say Mr Sharma bought a ULIP paying an annual premium of Rs.1 lakh. The sum
assured is Rs.10 lakhs. Mr Sharma died in the 10th year of the policy when the fund value was
Rs.12 lakhs. In this case, ULIPs will usually pay the higher of the sum assured or the fund value,
which is Rs.12 lakhs, as a death benefit. However, if Mr Sharma had opted for the Sum Assured
plus Fund Value ULIP, the death benefit would be Rs.22 lakhs (Rs.10 lakhs + Rs.12 lakhs).
What Happens to ULIPs After Maturity?
Once the plan matures, the coverage stops. You get the maturity benefits and the coverage is terminated.
However, if you have chosen the settlement option, your fund value stays invested in the selected funds.
The coverage does not continue but you can earn market-linked investment returns over the next five
years when you withdraw the corpus systematically under the settlement option.
How to Claim a Maturity Benefit?
In the case of maturity, the insurance company usually starts preparing for the claim payment before the
maturity date. On the day of maturity the company will process the maturity amount and initiate the
payment. Ensure your bank details are recorded with the insurer for hassle-free payout8.
Taxation on Maturity & Death Benefit7
The death benefit paid out under a ULIP plan is exempt from taxation.
The maturity benefit of a ULIP has specific taxation rules that you need to know. Here are the applicable
rules –
● If you had bought the ULIP before 1st February 2021, the maturity proceeds would be
tax-free if the premium is up to 10% of the death sum assured—section 10(10D) of the Income
Tax Act, 1961 grants this exemption.
● If you bought the ULIP on or after 1st February 2021, the maturity benefit would be tax-
free, provided the aggregate annual premium under all ULIPs is up to Rs.2.5 lakhs and policy is
satisfying Section 10(10D) conditions as mentioned there under.
● If the annual premium exceeds Rs.2.5 lakhs and the ULIP is bought on or after 1st
February 2021, long term capital gains tax would apply. Here’s how –
○ If you had invested the premium in debt-oriented funds, the earned returns would be
taxed at 20% without the benefit of indexation.
○ For equity-oriented funds, tax exemption is allowed for returns going up to Rs.1 lakh.
Returns over Rs.1 lakh are taxed at 10%.
So, understand the death and maturity benefits payable under ULIPs and their tax implications. The death
benefit is always tax-free irrespective of the premium amount7, while the maturity benefit might be
taxable depending on your policy details. So, know the benefits so that you can plan for your financial
goals accordingly.
Rules Regarding the Maturity Benefits of a ULIP
Besides the tax regulation, other factors also govern the maturity benefits of ULIPs in India:
1. Lock-In Period: ULIPs have a mandatory lock-in period of five years, during which
policyholders are not permitted to partially withdraw funds. It encourages long-term investment,
allowing the fund value to grow with market-linked returns. 6
Revival of Discontinued Policies: If premiums are not paid within the grace period, the policy is discontinued and risk cover ceases immediately. For ULIPs issued on or after 01‑09‑2010 with a 5‑year lock‑in, the fund value (after charges) is moved to a discontinued policy fund. The policyholder may revive within two years, but if not revived, proceeds are payable only after completion of the lock‑in. For policies discontinued after the lock‑in period, revival is allowed
within the insurer’s permitted window; if not revived, the policy terminates and surrender value is paid. Some insurers may, if opted by the policyholder, continue risk cover by deducting charges until the fund value reduces to one year’s premium, after which the policy terminates and payout is made.
Key Takeaways
● Upon maturity, ULIPs provide policyholders with the accumulated fund value, which can
be received as a lump sum or in regular intervals.
● Tax implications vary: Death benefits are always exempt from tax. However, maturity
benefits are tax-free only if the policy satisfies the premium-to-sum-assured conditions
mentioned in Section 10(10D). 7
● ULIPs offer flexibility through partial withdrawals, fund switching, , allowing individuals to
manage risk and optimize returns according to market conditions. 8
Conclusion
When a ULIP reaches maturity, both the life insurance coverage and the market-linked investment
component come to an end. After maturity, only the fund value is payable (either as a lump sum or
through the settlement option). No life cover continues beyond that point. Policyholders need to assess
these points to make the best use of ULIPs and strengthen their overall financial planning.
FAQs
1. Can I stay invested in ULIP after maturity?
You can’t stay invested in a ULIP (Unit Linked Insurance Plan) after it matures. Once the policy reaches its
maturity date, it ends, and the accumulated fund value is paid to you. However, you can withdraw the
maturity benefit under ULIPs in a phased manner by choosing the settlement option wherein the maturity
benefit is periodically paid over a period of time (which shall not exceed 5 years from the date of maturity).
2. What is the return of ULIP after 10 years?
ULIP returns over 10 years largely depend on the type of market-linked fund you choose and how the
market performs during that time. Equity funds generally have the potential to give higher returns. Still,
they come with higher risk, while debt funds offer more stability and lower risk, making them suitable for
risk-averse individuals.
